query_results: Search results: --- SOURCE 1: Deep homology - Wikipedia --- URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deep_homology SUMMARY: Deep homology is used to describe cases where growth and differentiation processes are governed by genetic mechanisms that are homologous and deeply conserved. FULL CONTENT: ![](/static/images/icons/wikipedia.png) ![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg) ![The Free Encyclopedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-tagline-en.svg) ## Contents # Deep homology ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/PAX6_Phenotypes_Washington_etal_PLoSBiol_e1000247.png/500px-PAX6_Phenotypes_Washington_etal_PLoSBiol_e1000247.png) In [evolutionary developmental biology](/wiki/Evolutionary_developmental_biology "Evolutionary developmental biology"), the concept of **deep homology** is used to describe cases where [growth](/wiki/Cell_growth "Cell growth") and [differentiation](/wiki/Cellular_differentiation "Cellular differentiation") processes are governed by genetic mechanisms that are [homologous](/wiki/Homology_(biology)#Homology_of_sequences_in_genetics "Homology (biology)") and deeply [conserved](/wiki/Conserved_sequence "Conserved sequence") across a wide range of [species](/wiki/Species "Species"). ## History In 1822, the French zoologist [Étienne Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire](/wiki/%C3%89tienne_Geoffroy_Saint-Hilaire "Étienne Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire") dissected a [crayfish](/wiki/Crayfish "Crayfish"), discovering that its body is organised like a vertebrate's, but [inverted belly to back (dorsoventrally)](/wiki/Inversion_(evolutionary_biology) "Inversion (evolutionary biology)"):[[1]](#cite_note-Held2-1) I just found that all the soft organs, that is to say, the principal organs of life are found in crustaceans, and so in insects, in the same order, in the same relationships and with the same arrangement as their analogues in the high vertebrate animals ... What was my surprise, and I may add, my admiration, seeing [such] a rule ...[[1]](#cite_note-Held2-1) ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Genes_hox.jpeg/500px-Genes_hox.jpeg) Geoffroy's homology theory was denounced by the leading French zoologist of his day, [Georges Cuvier](/wiki/Georges_Cuvier "Georges Cuvier"), but in 1994, Geoffroy was shown to be correct.[[1]](#cite_note-Held2-1) In 1915, [Santiago Ramon y Cajal](/wiki/Santiago_Ramon_y_Cajal "Santiago Ramon y Cajal") mapped the neural connections of the optic lobes of a fly, finding that these resembled those of vertebrates.[[1]](#cite_note-Held2-1) In 1978, [Edward B. Lewis](/wiki/Edward_B._Lewis "Edward B. Lewis") helped to found [evolutionary developmental biology](/wiki/Evolutionary_developmental_biology "Evolutionary developmental biology"), discovering that [homeotic genes](/wiki/Homeosis "Homeosis") regulated embryonic development in fruit flies.[[1]](#cite_note-Held2-1) In 1997, the term deep homology first appeared in a paper by [Neil Shubin](/wiki/Neil_Shubin "Neil Shubin"), Cliff Tabin, and [Sean B. Carroll](/wiki/Sean_B._Carroll "Sean B. Carroll"), describing the apparent relatedness in genetic regulatory apparatuses which indicated evolutionary similarities in disparate animal features.[[2]](#cite_note-Shubin_Tabin_Carroll_1997-2) ## Difference from ordinary homology Whereas ordinary [homology](/wiki/Homology_(biology) "Homology (biology)") is seen in the pattern of structures such as limb bones of mammals that are evidently related, deep homology can apply to groups of animals that have quite dissimilar anatomy: vertebrates (with [endoskeletons](/wiki/Endoskeleton "Endoskeleton") made of [bone](/wiki/Bone "Bone") and [cartilage](/wiki/Cartilage "Cartilage")) and arthropods (with [exoskeletons](/wiki/Exoskeleton "Exoskeleton") made of [chitin](/wiki/Chitin "Chitin")) nevertheless have limbs that are constructed using similar recipes or "algorithms".[[2]](#cite_note-Shubin_Tabin_Carroll_1997-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Carroll66-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Within the [metazoa](/wiki/Metazoa "Metazoa"), [homeotic genes](/wiki/Homeotic_gene "Homeotic gene") control differentiation along major [body axes](/wiki/Body_axis "Body axis"), and [pax genes](/wiki/Pax_genes "Pax genes") (especially [PAX6](/wiki/PAX6 "PAX6")) help to control the development of the [eye](/wiki/Eye "Eye") and other [sensory organs](/wiki/Sensory_organ "Sensory organ"). The deep homology applies across widely separated groups, such as in the eyes of [mammals](/wiki/Mammals "Mammals") and the structurally quite different [compound eyes](/wiki/Compound_eye "Compound eye") of [insects](/wiki/Insects "Insects").[[3]](#cite_note-Carroll66-3) Similarly, [hox genes](/wiki/Hox_gene "Hox gene") help to form an animal's segmentation pattern. HoxA and HoxD, that regulate finger and toe formation in mice, control the development of [ray fins](/wiki/Actinopterygii "Actinopterygii") in [zebrafish](/wiki/Zebrafish "Zebrafish"); these structures had until then been considered non-homologous.[[6]](#cite_note-6) There is a possible deep homology among animals that use acoustic communication, such as songbirds and humans, which may share functional versions of the [FOXP2](/wiki/FOXP2 "FOXP2") gene.[[7]](#cite_note-7) ## In cancer stem cells In modern day [biology](/wiki/Biology "Biology"), the depth of understanding deep homology has evolved into focusing on the [molecular](/wiki/Molecular_biology "Molecular biology") and [genetic mechanisms](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics") and functions rather than simple [morphology](/wiki/Morphology_(biology) "Morphology (biology)"). [Cancer stem cells](/wiki/Cancer_stem_cell "Cancer stem cell") (CSCs) are a population of cells within a tumor that have the ability to self-renew and differentiate into different cell types, similar to normal [stem cells](/wiki/Stem_cell "Stem cell"). The stem cell theory of cancer suggests that there is a subpopulation of cells, referred to as cancer stem cells, that have certain characteristics that make them unique among other types of cells within a cancer. The traits that are included in CSCs are that they multiply indefinitely, are resistant to [chemotherapy](/wiki/Chemotherapy "Chemotherapy"), and are proposed to be responsible for relapse after therapy.[[8]](#cite_note-8) ### Life cycle of cancer The [unicellular life cycle](/wiki/Unicellular_organism "Unicellular organism") of cancer and [Entamoeba](/wiki/Entamoeba "Entamoeba") is uniquely similar, and thus contradicts the molecular phylostratigraphic theory for the origin of cancer.[[9]](#cite_note-9) This deep relationship between the two cell systems is supported by the "amoeba model", which provides a greater understanding of the biology of cancer from the evolutionary perspective.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) The G + S life cycle of [Entamoeba](/wiki/Entamoeba "Entamoeba") is the closest common ancestor than compared to any other life cycle of unicellular organisms. Similarly, both cell systems, [amoeba](/wiki/Amoeba "Amoeba") and [cancer](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer"), use the deep homologous G + S gene module that was evolved by a common ancestor. Some parallels that they share are too close for coincidence including: MGRSs are also known in medical terms as “pre-existing Polypoid Giant Cancer Cells (PGCCs)” and are frequently observed in untreated cancers.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In cancer, the reproductive [germ-line](/wiki/Germline "Germline") cycle starts with a precursor cell. This cell will then polyploidize within a cell envelope. This cancer germ-line undergoes a process of development that is similar to the Entamoeba germline. A significant trace of deep homology can be found in mammalian germ-line stem cells. Based on a previous hypothesis, the germ-line is the common ancestor in somatic stem cell lineages. Daughter GSCs are the only stem cells that have the capability of passing genetic information throughout generations.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) ## Algorithm In 2010, a team led by [Edward Marcotte](/wiki/Edward_Marcotte "Edward Marcotte") developed an [algorithm](/wiki/Algorithm "Algorithm") that identifies deeply homologous genetic modules in unicellular organisms, plants, and animals based on [phenotypes](/wiki/Phenotype "Phenotype") (such as traits and developmental defects). The technique aligns phenotypes across organisms based on [orthology](/wiki/Homology_(biology)#Orthology "Homology (biology)") (a type of homology) of genes involved in the phenotypes.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-McGary2010-13) ## See also ## References ![](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:CentralAutoLogin/start?type=1x1&usesul3=1) ![Wikimedia Foundation](/static/images/footer/wikimedia.svg) ![Powered by MediaWiki](/w/resources/assets/mediawiki_compact.svg) -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --- SOURCE 2: Deep Homology? Uncanny Similarities of Humans and Flies ... --- URL: https://www.sdbonline.org/sites/fly/lewhelddeep/deephomology.htm SUMMARY: The field of evo-devo (evolutionary developmental biology) has recently been solving age-old mysteries of animal evolution by providing genetic explanations. FULL CONTENT: | | | | --- | --- | | ([figure legend](deephomology.htm#legend)) | Deep Homology? | ![](back.jpg) ![](next.jpg) | | | --- | | What follows when you select the 'next page' arrow (upper left on each page) are some of the uncanny homologies that exist between humans and fruit flies. All 18 figures are excerpted from the new book *Deep Homology?* published by [Cambridge University Press](http://www.cambridge.org/us/academic/subjects/life-sciences/evolutionary-biology/deep-homology-uncanny-similarities-humans-and-flies-uncovered-evo-devo?format=PB), January, 2017. The field of evo-devo (evolutionary developmental biology) has recently been solving age-old mysteries of animal evolution by providing genetic explanations. Key concepts from evo-devo are defined in the [glossary](https://www.sdbonline.org/sites/fly/lewheldsnake/heldsnake15.htm), included in excerpts from Held's book, *How the Snake Lost Its Legs*. Use the arrows (or the index, below) to move forward or back among the slides. **Humans versus flies.** Humans and fruit flies look nothing alike, but they construct their anatomies using remarkably similar genetic circuitry. Much of that circuitry was inherited from a common ancestor, which lived ~600 million years ago. The greater the intricacy of the shared circuitry, the stronger the case for deep homology, versus alternative explanations (parallel co-option or convergent evolution). The purpose of the "compare and contrast" exercise presented here is two-fold: (1) to assess the extent to which humans and flies use the same circuits, and (2) to survey as many animal phyla as possible to determine how widely the shared mechanisms have been conserved throughout animal evolution. [*Deep Homology? Uncanny Similarities of Humans and Flies Uncovered by Evo-Devo*](http://www.cambridge.org/us/academic/subjects/life-sciences/evolutionary-biology/deep-homology-uncanny-similarities-humans-and-flies-uncovered-evo-devo?format=PB) Reprinted with permission, © Cambridge University Press Figure legends are abbreviated. References have been omitted for clarity. | | Introduction: cover image Body axes: [figure 2](deephomology02.htm) | [figure 3](deephomology03.htm) | [figure 4](deephomology04.htm) | [figure 5](deephomology05.htm) | [figure 6](deephomology06.htm) Nervous system: [figure 7](deephomology07.htm) | [figure 8](deephomology08.htm) Vision: [figure 9](deephomology09.htm) | [figure 10](deephomology10.htm) | [figure 11](deephomology11.htm) | [figure 12](deephomology12.htm) | [figure 13](deephomology13.htm) Touch and hearing: [figure 14](deephomology14.htm) | [figure 15](deephomology15.htm) Smell and taste: [figure 16](deephomology16.htm) Limbs: [figure 17](deephomology17.htm) Epilogue: [figure 18](deephomology18.htm) The Inter*active* Fly resides on the web server of the [Society for Developmental Biology](http://sdbonline.org/). | ![](dh01.jpg) ![](next.jpg) The Inter*active* Fly resides on the web server of the [Society for Developmental Biology](http://sdbonline.org/). -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --- SOURCE 3: Deep homology: a view from systematics - PubMed --- URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20394064/ SUMMARY: Deep homology is part of a much wider range of phenomena in which biological (genes, regulatory mechanisms, morphological traits) and phylogenetic levels of FULL CONTENT: ![U.S. flag](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/coreutils/uswds/img/favicons/favicon-57.png) An official website of the United States government ![Dot gov](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/coreutils/uswds/img/icon-dot-gov.svg) **The .gov means it’s official.** Federal government websites often end in .gov or .mil. Before sharing sensitive information, make sure you’re on a federal government site. ![Https](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/coreutils/uswds/img/icon-https.svg) **The site is secure.** The **https://** ensures that you are connecting to the official website and that any information you provide is encrypted and transmitted securely. ![NIH NLM Logo](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/coreutils/nwds/img/logos/AgencyLogo.svg) #### Account ![pubmed logo](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/37b1c497-d124-4838-b4a6-d9a1b84e1840/core/images/pubmed-logo-blue.svg) ## Save citation to file ## Email citation ### Add to Collections ### Add to My Bibliography ## Your saved search ## Create a file for external citation management software ## Your RSS Feed ### Full text links ![Wiley full text link](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/corehtml/query/egifs/http:--media.wiley.com-assets-7388-69-wiley-full-text.png) ### Actions ### Page navigation # Deep homology: a view from systematics ### Affiliation # Deep homology: a view from systematics ### Author ### Affiliation ## Abstract Over the past decade, it has been discovered that disparate aspects of morphology - often of distantly related groups of organisms - are regulated by the same genetic regulatory mechanisms. Those discoveries provide a new perspective on morphological evolutionary change. A conceptual framework for exploring these research findings is termed 'deep homology'. A comparative framework for morphological relations of homology is provided that distinguishes analogy, homoplasy, plesiomorphy and synapomorphy. Four examples - three from plants and one from animals - demonstrate that homologous developmental mechanisms can regulate a range of morphological relations including analogy, homoplasy and examples of uncertain homology. Deep homology is part of a much wider range of phenomena in which biological (genes, regulatory mechanisms, morphological traits) and phylogenetic levels of homology can both be disassociated. Therefore, to understand homology, precise, comparative, independent statements of both biological and phylogenetic levels of homology are necessary. Known for its critical role in the formation of eyes of the ever-present fruit FULL CONTENT: [![theGIST](https://the-gist.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/he-gist.png)](https://the-gist.org/ "theGIST | Home page") ![theGIST](https://the-gist.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/he-gist.png) [![theGIST](https://the-gist.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/he-gist.png)](https://the-gist.org/ "theGIST | Home page") ![theGIST](https://the-gist.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/he-gist.png) # Going Down the Rabbit Hole of Evo-devo: Deep Homology by [samuelclough](https://the-gist.org/author/cap-samuelclough/ "Posts by samuelclough") · February 22, 2018 ![](https://the-gist.org/wp-content/uploads/2018/02/6850446665_50a95dd8d5_b-1024x500.jpg) Evo-devo is short for the fusion of two disciplines: evolutionary and developmental biology. Investigating the earliest stages of the development of living organisms is the focus of evo-devo, which reveals how similar all living things are[1](#easy-footnote-bottom-1-9147 " http://www.pbs.org/wgbh/nova/evolution/what-evo-devo.html "). The cornerstone holding this fascinating discipline together is ’deep homology’, which may sound like a niche mathematical concept, but it’s actually quite simple. All living beings develop in a particular manner that is invariant across broad groups of organisms – this is due to the fact these creatures all descended from the same common ancestor that developed like that. A simple way to showcase deep homology is to use the PAX6 gene. Known for its critical role in the formation of eyes of the ever-present fruit fly; additionally, fruitless fruit flies with non-functional PAX6 genes are blind[2](#easy-footnote-bottom-2-9147 " http://bioteaching.com/deep-homology/ "). The weirdest thing about PAX6 is that a transfer of the PAX6 protein from a mouse can sustain the development of the eye in fruit flies, which indicates that PAX6 has a similar function across seemingly unrelated animals. Fruit flies and mouse eyes develop in similar ways due to their last shared ancestor having this gene, which harkens back aeons ago when primordial animals looked more like plants. A more complex example would be the genes that develop the back and front of a fruit fly. We have the same genes as the fruit fly for development of the back and front, however, with a significant difference that it occurs upside down for the fly. Or the right way up if you take the perspective of the fly. What is the significance of these findings? The clue is that genetic differences alone cannot account for the observed differences of the species. We share roughly 60% of our genes with the fruit fly, but thankfully, we look nothing like them[3](#easy-footnote-bottom-3-9147 " https://www.theguardian.com/science/2017/oct/07/fruit-fly-fascination-nobel-prizes-genetics "). Something is missing, and so to account for the vast diversity of life, we need to factor in what deep homology tells us – that our development is crucial. More specifically, what genes turn on and off and how all they interact with each other during development: this is how we differ from the fruit fly, even if many of us also tend to hover around the kitchen hunting for food[4](#easy-footnote-bottom-4-9147 " http://www.pbs.org/wgbh/nova/evolution/what-evo-devo.html "). Without the slight changes during the brief development period, we wouldn’t have the rich variety of the natural world. To surmise, it’s the subtleties that make life interesting. *Edited by Richard Murchie* ## Author ![samuelclough](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/?s=80&d=identicon&r=g) #### References Tags: [Biology](https://the-gist.org/tag/biology/)[developmental](https://the-gist.org/tag/developmental/)[evolution](https://the-gist.org/tag/evolution/)[Genetics](https://the-gist.org/tag/genetics/) #### You may also like... ![](https://the-gist.org/wp-content/uploads/2013/12/34.-Tim-Revell.jpg) #### [Foamover FINALLY Understood](https://the-gist.org/2013/12/foamover-finally-understood/) December 14, 2013 ![Transparent avatar with brain showing](https://the-gist.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/Image-1-520x245.png) #### [The future of stroke: Is a new public health philosophy needed?](https://the-gist.org/2020/05/the-future-of-stroke-is-a-new-public-health-philosophy-needed/) May 26, 2020 ![](https://the-gist.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/01/Frosty-lab-image-520x245.jpg) #### [Observing the cosmos from deep within Antarctic Ice](https://the-gist.org/2022/01/observing-the-cosmos-from-deep-within-antarctic-ice/) January 15, 2022 ### Leave a Reply [Cancel reply](/2018/02/going-rabbit-hole-evo-devo-deep-homology/#respond) Your email address will not be published. IMAGE 5: URL: https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Lauren-Oconnell-5/publication/237094152/figure/fig3/AS:299527954288650@1448424437315/Evolution-of-neuroethological-systems-A-Deep-homology-refers-to-conserved-phenotypes_Q320.jpg DESCRIPTION: None --------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Deep homology reveals conserved genetic mechanisms across diverse species, demonstrating that similar developmental processes can arise from shared ancestral genes, despite anatomical differences.
Gene regulatory networks orchestrate the expression of genes during development, illustrating how intricate interactions can lead to diverse morphological outcomes across various organisms.

Developmental plasticity refers to the ability of organisms to adapt their developmental pathways in response to environmental changes, highlighting the evolutionary significance of flexible developmental strategies.
Understanding deep homology and gene networks provides insights into evolutionary processes, revealing how similar developmental pathways can lead to the diversification of species over time.

Ecological interactions influence evolutionary developmental biology by shaping the adaptations organisms develop, reflecting the dynamic relationship between environment and genetic regulatory mechanisms.
